summary: Gruber dismantles Think Secret's argument that Apple's trade secret lawsuit is merely a bullying tactic against a small publication. He admits his own initial legal analysis was wrong — Apple's case rests on trade secret law, not NDA enforcement, and is likely strong. He specifically targets Think Secret's claim that Apple would never sue The New York Times for publishing the same information, arguing this comparison is nonsensical because the Times would never publish unsubstantiated product rumors in the first place. He further undercuts Think Secret's credibility by documenting their refusal to issue corrections when their iPod Mini color screen prediction proved completely wrong. Gruber concludes that Apple would actually be more likely to sue a major publication, since it would avoid the David-vs-Goliath optics.
key_points: Apple's case against Think Secret is based on trade secret law, not NDA enforcement — a much stronger legal foundation than most commentators assumed
The comparison to The New York Times is fallacious because the Times would never publish unsubstantiated product rumors in the first place
Think Secret's refusal to issue corrections when proven wrong (e.g., the iPod Mini color screen prediction) undermines their claim to legitimate journalism
Apple would likely be more inclined to sue a deep-pocketed publication because it would eliminate the negative David-vs-Goliath publicity
Gruber openly corrects his own earlier analysis, modeling the intellectual honesty he finds lacking in Think Secret
money_quotes: First and foremost, not only *didn't* The New York Times publish such information, they *wouldn't*.
The Times doesn't print unsubstantiated or dubiously-sourced gossipy conjecture on unannounced or unreleased products. That's why they're The New York Times.
Most publications, including The New York Times, would consider a report that "contradicts with reality" to be, you know, an *error*, and thus in need of correction.
At Think Secret, it's good enough that "some" of what they published was "in line with" reality.
My initial armchair legal analysis was way off-base, however.

If The New York Times, or any other deep-pocketed mainstream publication, had published the same information, obtained in the same way, Apple might have been *more* likely to file suit than they were against Think Secret.
A lawsuit against The New York Times would be seen as Apple picking on someone its own size.
When you hear someone claim that if they did, Apple wouldn't sue them, don't believe it.
key_insight: Think Secret's invocation of The New York Times as a shield is doubly hollow: the Times would never publish such material, and if it did, Apple would have even more reason to sue.
